cataloging_subject.label: A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century, Containing a Religious Play and Poetry, Legal Forms, and Local Accounts
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition-defined composite selection from the Book of Brome: selected medieval literary, devotional, legal, administrative, accounting, and medical components rearranged into three topical parts. It is not one medieval authored work, not the whole Yale codex, and not merely a physical book or scan. Stadolnik's later description of the source codex as a miscellany informs interpretation without replacing the edition's printed title or boundary.

catalog_summary.title: A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century, Containing a Religious Play and Poetry, Legal Forms, and Local Accounts
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous and attributed medieval writers, scribes, and documentary compilers
catalog_summary.date: Selected witness texts chiefly 1475–1500, with Robert Melton's additions c. 1499–1508; edited selection published 1886
catalog_summary.region: England, especially East Anglia and the Suffolk–Norfolk border

The Annunciation, Incarnation, and Marian devotion
Property, landholding, leases, and estate management

summaries.value: Lucy Toulmin Smith's 1886 edition rearranges selected contents of the late-fifteenth- and early-sixteenth-century Book of Brome into poetry and puzzles, manorial law, and private accounts. Its literary components include conduct verse, a dice prognostic, Adrian and Epotys, the Brome Abraham and Isaac play, the Fifteen Signs, St Patrick's Purgatory, a fragmentary Life of St Margaret, prayers, a trental direction, and an Annunciation carol. Administrative material includes common and tax lists, church duties, twelve paired-language legal model documents, court articles, accounts, a 1504 agreement, and an incomplete jaundice recipe.

The edition has three zero-count topical parts and twenty-four zero-count edition sections. A count-once model recognizes forty-seven intellectual components or clusters: twenty in Part 1, twenty in Part 2, and seven in Part 3. Five separately headed cipher puzzles count as five; twelve numbered legal models count as twelve while their Latin/English streams count once per model; stanza, outcome, table-row, and editorial comparison subdivisions do not add works.

This record describes the edition-defined selection. Yale's current catalog has twenty-nine manuscript articles, of which Smith selected articles 1–4 and 6–28 and rearranged them into twenty-four sections. Neither twenty-seven selected witness articles nor twenty-four DIV2 sections is used as the intellectual-item total; the explicit count-bearing descendants in content_parts reconcile to forty-seven.

Joe Stadolnik argues that 'miscellany' better describes the source codex than 'commonplace book' and distinguishes a professional impersonal compilation from Robert Melton's later personal additions. This later interpretation informs the witness model but does not replace the printed 1886 title or the edition-defined catalog boundary.

extent.description: Count-once intellectual extent: 47 components or clusters = 35 prose/list/legal/account items + 12 verse compositions, allocated 20/20/7 across the three topical parts. The 3 parts and 24 DIV2 sections are structural and count zero. Raw XML axes remain distinct: 6 DIV1, 24 DIV2, 25 DIV3, 168 LG, and 2,799 L. Excluding 2 LG/17 L in ARGUMENT streams and 6 LG/14 L nested inside outer LG/L lines as variant-note streams yields 160 main LG and 2,768 main L. LG/L are encoding/layout counts and include outcome groups or account lines, so they do not determine the 47-item intellectual count.

content_parts.label: Part 1: Poetry, including a few puzzles and sayings
content_parts.summary: Smith's first topical part combines verse, a play, prayers, ciphers, lists, and one prose trental direction; it is not the manuscript's physical first block.
notes: Topical container only; its descendants carry the count-once intellectual extent.

content_parts.label: Some Old Puzzles and Sayings
content_parts.summary: One saying stanza, one positions list, five separately headed puzzles, and one Daily Rules stanza.
notes: Edition/encoding section container; contributes zero to the intellectual item count.

content_parts.label: Sayings
content_parts.summary: Four-line saying stanza beginning with the hart's love of the wood.

content_parts.label: Five puzzles
content_parts.summary: Five separately headed cipher puzzles; the source heading supplies the exact cluster count.
notes: The five puzzle tables count once each; decoded and cipher columns are parallel representations, not ten items.

evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded title page, preface, three-part arrangement, selected texts, editorial notes, page and folio milestones, and repository identifier.

evidence.citation: Linear element, ancestry, heading, and source-structure inspection of repository ajd3529.xml.
evidence.locator: DIV1/DIV2/DIV3, HEAD, L, LG, P, TABLE, PB, NOTE, and folio MILESTONE elements
evidence.summary: Confirms raw 6/24/25 DIV1/DIV2/DIV3, 168 LG, 2,799 L, and 188 PB; excluding 2 LG/17 L in ARGUMENT streams and 6 LG/14 L nested inside outer LG/L lines as variant-note streams gives 160 main LG and 2,768 main L; supports the explicit 47-component count-once model and printed-page locators.

evidence.citation: Lucy Toulmin Smith, ed., A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century (London and Norwich, 1886).
evidence.summary: Complete scan confirming title, half-title, responsibilities, publication, three-part selection, component headings, editorial interventions, pagination, and two partial facsimiles.

evidence.citation: Lucy Toulmin Smith, preface to A Common-place Book of the Fifteenth Century (1886).
evidence.summary: Explains Kerrison's commissioning role, Smith's editorial work, topical rearrangement, transcription and collation practices, and scope.

evidence.citation: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Book of Brome, Beinecke MS 365.
evidence.summary: Confirms current witness identity, date, hands, 29-article contents, exact folio loci, physical description, provenance, and access conditions.

evidence.citation: Joe Stadolnik, 'The Brome Abraham and Isaac and Impersonal Compilation' (2019).
evidence.summary: Supports the modern miscellany terminology and two-stage professional/personal compilation interpretation.

open_questions.question: Does the title-page phrase 'Originally printed for private circulation' refer to a distinct earlier issue or state, and if so how is it related to the 1886 Trübner/Goose issue?
next_steps: Consult publisher archives, bibliographies, and multiple physical copies for issue/state evidence.
notes: The phrase is preserved without asserting a separate edition.

open_questions.question: Which exact comparison manuscript supplied Smith's 'variaunce' reading in the Lydgate extract?
next_steps: Collate Harley 116 f.124 and Harley 2251 f.173 when images or reproductions can be consulted.

open_questions.question: Should a future corpus-wide policy split or aggregate any of the 47 edition-defined components differently?
next_steps: Retain the explicit count-bearing descendants and revise only if a documented cross-corpus counting policy supersedes this count-once model.
notes: The present exact total is defensible from source headings and content boundaries; this question records policy comparability, not factual uncertainty in the raw axes.
Smith's selection uses current Yale articles 1–4 and 6–28; articles 5 and 29 are not selected.
The count-bearing content_parts sum exactly to 47; all topical parts and edition/encoding section containers have item_count 0.
Stadolnik's 'miscellany' terminology applies to interpretation of the source codex and does not replace the printed preferred title.
